--

While building enterprise applications, one issue I frequently encountered was users accidentally triggering the same API multiple times.
This usually happened when users clicked buttons such as Save, Update, or Search repeatedly before the first request completed.
Although this may seem like a small issue, duplicate API requests can create significant problems in production systems.
The Problem
Consider a scenario where a user clicks the Save button multiple times because the API response takes a few seconds.
This can result in:

• Duplicate records being created

• Unnecessary backend processing

• Increased server load

• Poor user experience

• Data inconsistencies
In healthcare applications, duplicate operations can become even more problematic because they may affect patient records, appointments, billing, or clinical workflows.
The Solution
To avoid duplicate API requests, I implemented a simple but effective approach:

• Disable the action button immediately after the request starts.

• Re-enable it once the API request completes successfully or fails.

• Ignore additional clicks while the request is in progress.
Example Implementation
Component
isSaving = false;savePatient() { if (this.isSaving) {
return;
} this.isSaving = true; this.patientService.save(this.form.value).subscribe({
next: (response) => {
console.log('Saved Successfully');
this.isSaving = false;
},
error: () => {
this.isSaving = false;
}
});
}Template
<button
[disabled]="isSaving"
(click)="savePatient()">
Save
</button>With this implementation, users cannot trigger multiple requests while the previous request is still being processed.
Before Optimization

• Multiple requests were sent for the same action.

• Duplicate records could be created.

• Backend processing increased unnecessarily.
After Optimization

• Only a single request was processed at a time.

• Duplicate submissions were eliminated.

• User experience became more predictable and reliable.
Going One Step Further with RxJS
Angular and RxJS provide operators that can further help manage duplicate requests.
For example, exhaustMap() ignores new requests until the current request completes.
saveClicks$
.pipe(
exhaustMap(() =>
this.patientService.save(this.form.value)
)
)
.subscribe();exhaustMap() is particularly useful for form submissions because it ignores subsequent requests until the current request completes, making it ideal for preventing duplicate actions.
This approach is especially useful for:

• Form submissions

• Payment processing

• Appointment booking

• Record creation workflows
Real-World Scenario
In one of the healthcare applications I worked on, during periods of slower network response, users occasionally clicked Save or Update multiple times because they were unsure whether the action had been processed successfully.
This resulted in duplicate API calls and unnecessary backend processing.
By introducing button disabling and request handling mechanisms, we were able to:

• Eliminate duplicate submissions

• Improve data consistency

• Reduce unnecessary backend traffic

• Improve overall user experience
When Should You Apply This?
This pattern is highly recommended for:
✅ Save operations
✅ Update operations
✅ Payment processing
✅ Appointment booking
✅ Search operations with heavy processing
✅ File uploads
Key Takeaway
Preventing duplicate API requests is a small optimization that can have a significant impact on system reliability and user experience.
Sometimes improving an application’s quality does not require major architectural changes. Small defensive programming practices like request throttling and button state management can prevent production issues before they occur.
As developers, building features is important — but building resilient, reliable, and user-friendly applications is what creates great experiences for both users and businesses.
